mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, remember this. The U S government forced Anthropic to switch off its two most powerful models for all foreign users. And within days, Chinese labs answered by giving theirs away for free. The A I race just turned into an A I sovereignty race.

Number one. Anthropic pulls Mythos five and Fable five after a U S order. Late Friday, Anthropic received a directive from the Commerce Department. A letter from Secretary Howard Lutnick to chief executive Dario Amodei ordered it to cut off access to its Fable five and Mythos five models for any foreign national, inside or outside the country, on national security grounds. The trigger was a newly discovered jailbreak of Fable five. Anthropic disabled both models for all customers, while disagreeing that one narrow exploit justified recalling a product used by hundreds of millions of people. Its other models stay live, and the Justice Department is now appealing a court order that paused the ban. This came from your Reads. The Rundown, The A I Collective, and Tech Policy Press. Why it matters. It's the first time Washington has reached in and switched off a commercial frontier model. A precedent every A I company is now reading very carefully.

Number two. China's labs answer by opening their models. Your recode China A I newsletter reports that MiniMax, Zhipu, and Moonshot moved fast on the Anthropic news. They released M three, G L M five point two, and K two point seven Code, and made them openly available. The pitch to global developers locked out of Mythos and Fable is simple. Come use ours instead. No border check. Why it matters. Export controls meant to contain rivals can also hand them the customers. Openness is becoming China's competitive weapon.

Number three. Apple's new Siri runs on Google. From The A I Collective. At its developer conference, Apple showed a rebuilt Siri. And under the hood, it's Google's Gemini, through a partnership reportedly worth about one billion dollars a year. Apple gets a custom model with one point two trillion parameters for Siri and Apple Intelligence, rolling out to roughly one and a half billion users with the next i O S update. Why it matters. The most controlling company in tech just outsourced the brain of its assistant. A quiet admission of how far behind it fell on A I.

Number four. Google ships Gemini three point five Flash into a crowded launch wave. Google released Gemini three point five Flash, tuned for agent and coding work. It beats its own three point one Pro while running about four times faster, with three point five Pro due next month. Google also launched Gemini Omni for video generation. All of this lands in a June already crowded with Claude Mythos one, a rumored Sonnet four point eight, and x A I's long-delayed Grok five. Why it matters. Frontier models are now shipping almost weekly. The story is shifting from raw capability to speed, cost, and who can deploy fastest.

Number five. Open A I eyes a record public offering. Open A I is preparing to confidentially file for an initial public offering in the coming weeks, on a private valuation of roughly seven hundred thirty billion dollars, with a listing possible as soon as September. Why it matters. A public Open A I would be one of the largest tech debuts ever. And a real-time market test of whether A I's sky-high valuations hold up.

Number six. The chip flow stays stuck. On the geopolitics front. A rule from January shifted Nvidia's H two hundred and A M D's M I three twenty-five X exports to China from outright denial to case-by-case licensing. But shipments are still a trickle, as Washington and Beijing argue over security terms. Congress keeps pushing to tighten things through the Chip Security Act. Why it matters. Policy loosened on paper but froze in practice. The hardware bottleneck driving the whole A I map is still clamped shut.

Number seven. The media's bid to make A I pay for news. This one's close to your world in comms. There's a growing global push for statutory licensing that would require A I companies to pay publishers for journalism used to train their models, past and future. Separately, Colorado's A I Act, with rules for high-risk systems, is set to take effect June thirtieth. Why it matters. If pay for news becomes law, it rewires the economics between every newsroom and every A I lab overnight.

And now the good news. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
